enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted genealogy software for building, editing, and sharing family trees collaboratively from GEDCOM data.
enrichment.seo_title: webtrees Review: Free Open-Source Genealogy Software (2026)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what webtrees offers: free self-hosted genealogy software with GEDCOM support, family tree charts, privacy controls, and 40+ languages. Features, pricing, FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: webtrees is a free, open-source, web-based genealogy application that lets individuals, families, and genealogical societies build, edit, and publish family trees on their own web server. First released around 2010 as a successor to the earlier PhpGedView project, webtrees is licensed under the GNU GPL version 3 and developed by a community of volunteer contributors from many countries. Because it is self-hosted rather than delivered as a SaaS product, using webtrees costs nothing beyond standard PHP and database web hosting (MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, or SQLite are supported), and users retain full ownership and control of their genealogical data rather than storing it with a commercial provider like Ancestry or MyHeritage. The software works from standard GEDCOM files (compatible with GEDCOM 5.5.1 and newer 7.x specifications), so trees created in desktop genealogy applications or exported from other online services can be imported directly. Core functionality includes full editing of individuals, families, sources, and media records; multi-user accounts so relatives around the world can collaborate on a shared tree; and granular, per-record privacy controls that can automatically restrict details about living individuals. webtrees generates a range of charts and reports, including pedigree, descendancy, relationship, fan, and family book views, along with timelines and statistics. It supports interactive maps (via Google Maps or similar services) to plot where ancestors were born, married, or died, and it includes media galleries for attaching scanned photos, certificates, and documents to tree records. The interface is available in more than 40 languages and supports diverse cultural naming conventions, including patronymic, matronymic, and surname-first traditions. webtrees is extensible through a module system and a selection of official and community-built themes, and it integrates research tools such as journals, to-do lists, and census assistants. It has no built-in commercial record-hint database like paid competitors, so it functions purely as a tree-building, editing, and publishing platform rather than a genealogical records search service. Typical users include genealogical societies hosting shared member trees, families publishing their history online, and individual researchers who prefer to self-host rather than depend on a subscription-based platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is webtrees
paragraphs: webtrees is a free, open-source genealogy application that runs on your own web server, letting you build, edit, and publish family trees online. It grew out of the earlier PhpGedView project and has been maintained by an international community of volunteer developers since around 2010.

Because webtrees is self-hosted rather than a paid cloud service, there are no subscription fees and no third party controls your family data. All that is required is a web host running PHP with a supported database such as MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, or SQLite.

The software is built around the GEDCOM standard, the common file format used by nearly all genealogy programs, which makes it straightforward to import an existing tree from desktop software or another online service and continue building it in webtrees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: webtrees supports multi-user collaboration, so several relatives can log in and contribute research to the same shared tree, each with configurable permission levels.
Privacy is handled through granular, per-record controls that can automatically hide sensitive details about living individuals, which matters for trees that will be viewed publicly online.

The platform produces a wide variety of charts and reports, including pedigree, descendancy, relationship, and fan charts, plus interactive maps that plot ancestral birth, marriage, and death locations, and media galleries for photos and scanned documents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: webtrees itself is completely free to download, install, and use under the GPL version 3 license. There is no paid tier, subscription, or per-user fee for the software.

The only cost involved is standard web hosting for the PHP application and its database, which can range from a few dollars a month on shared hosting to more for dedicated or managed genealogy-specific hosting providers.

Some third-party companies offer managed webtrees hosting or paid support contracts for users who prefer not to self-manage the server, but these are optional add-ons rather than requirements from the webtrees project itself.

features.title: Media Galleries

features.description: Plot ancestor birth, marriage, and death locations on a map to visualize family migration over time.

features.description: Interface available in more than 40 languages with support for diverse surname and naming conventions.

features.description: Customizable themes and an extensible module system supported by the open-source community.

pros: Completely free with no subscription or per-user fees
Full data ownership since the tree is self-hosted on your own server
Strong multi-user collaboration with granular privacy controls
Active open-source community with regular updates
Wide GEDCOM compatibility with major desktop genealogy applications
cons: Requires technical setup on PHP and database web hosting, which is not beginner-friendly
No built-in historical record hints or search database like paid competitors
Interface feels dated compared to modern commercial genealogy platforms
No official native mobile app; relies on a responsive web interface

faqs.question: Is webtrees really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, webtrees is released under the GPL version 3 license and is free to download, install, and modify. You only pay for your own web hosting.

faqs.question: Do I need technical skills to install webtrees?
faqs.answer: You need a web host that supports PHP and a database such as MySQL or PostgreSQL. Some genealogy-focused hosts offer simplified or one-click installs.

faqs.question: Can I import my existing family tree?
faqs.answer: Yes, webtrees imports standard GEDCOM files exported from desktop genealogy software or other online tree services.

faqs.question: Does webtrees protect living relatives' privacy?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes granular privacy settings that can hide or mask details of living individuals by default.

faqs.question: Can multiple family members edit the same tree?
faqs.answer: Yes, webtrees supports multi-user accounts with configurable permission levels for collaborative editing.

faqs.question: What languages does webtrees support?
faqs.answer: The interface is available in more than 40 languages and supports various cultural naming and surname conventions.

faqs.question: Does webtrees offer historical record search like commercial services?
faqs.answer: No, webtrees is a tree-building and publishing platform, not a historical records database.

faqs.question: Is there official support for webtrees?
faqs.answer: Community support is available through forums, and some third-party hosting providers offer paid managed support.

enrichment.short_description: Drag-and-drop website and online store builder, now owned by Square, aimed at small businesses and entrepreneurs.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (paid plans from about $10/month billed annually)
enrichment.seo_title: Weebly Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Square Integration
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Weebly overview: drag-and-drop website builder pricing plans, built-in ecommerce, Square payments integration, pros, cons, and frequently asked questions.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Weebly is a drag-and-drop website and online store builder founded in 2006 by David Rusenko, Chris Fanini, and Dan Veltri, who began building the product while at Pennsylvania State University. The company is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and was acquired by payments company Square (now Block, Inc.) in 2018, which has since deepened Weebly's focus on ecommerce and payments for small businesses. Weebly targets non-technical small business owners, freelancers, and entrepreneurs who want to launch a website or online store quickly without writing code. The core product is a visual, drag-and-drop site editor with mobile-responsive themes, along with built-in ecommerce functionality that is available on every plan tier, including the permanent free plan, which is unusual among mainstream website builders. Because Weebly is owned by Square, it offers tight integration with Square's payment processing and point-of-sale system, letting merchants sync online and in-person sales, inventory, and customer data in one place. Weebly's pricing structure includes a free tier that carries Weebly and Square branding and does not support a custom domain, a Personal plan priced around $10 to $15 per month, a Professional plan around $12 per month billed annually that adds a free custom domain for the first year and Google Ads credit, and a Performance plan at roughly $26 per month for higher-volume stores that removes Weebly's platform transaction fee. Additional features include a free SSL certificate on all plans, basic SEO tools, email marketing capabilities, a blogging engine, and an app center for extending functionality with third-party integrations. Compared to more design-flexible builders like Webflow or Wix, Weebly trades some layout customization for simplicity and speed, making it best suited to straightforward small business or local service websites and modest online stores rather than complex, highly custom sites or large-scale ecommerce operations that might be better served by a platform like Shopify.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Weebly
paragraphs: Weebly is a drag-and-drop website builder that lets small business owners, freelancers, and entrepreneurs create websites and online stores without writing code. It was founded in 2006 and is now owned by Square, giving it particularly strong ties to Square's payments and point-of-sale ecosystem.

The platform is built around simplicity: a visual editor with mobile-responsive themes lets users assemble pages by dragging elements into place, making it a common choice for people building their first website.
Since the Square acquisition, ecommerce has become a central focus, with built-in online store functionality available on every plan, including the free tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Weebly's drag-and-drop editor covers page layout, image galleries, forms, and blogging, all wrapped in mobile-responsive themes that require no manual adjustment for different screen sizes.

Built-in ecommerce lets store owners list physical and digital products, manage inventory, and process payments without installing third-party apps, and it integrates directly with Square for in-person and online sales syncing.
Additional tools include basic SEO settings, email marketing features, an app center for extending site functionality, and a free SSL certificate included on every plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Weebly offers a permanent free plan with Weebly and Square branding, limited storage, and no custom domain support, alongside three paid tiers.
The Personal plan runs roughly $10 to $15 per month, and the Professional plan is around $12 per month when billed annually, adding a free custom domain for the first year and Google Ads credit.
The top-tier Performance plan costs about $26 per month and is aimed at higher-volume online stores, removing Weebly's platform transaction fee on sales.

features.description: HTTPS encryption included by default on every plan, including free.

Ecommerce features available even on the free plan

Affordable entry-level pricing for paid plans
Reliable hosting backed by Square and Block infrastructure
cons: Design flexibility is more limited than builders like Webflow or Wix for complex layouts
Free plan displays Weebly and Square branding with no custom domain
Fewer advanced ecommerce features than dedicated platforms like Shopify at scale
Smaller app and plugin ecosystem compared to WordPress or Wix

faqs.question: Is Weebly free?
faqs.answer: Yes, there is a permanent free plan with Weebly branding and no custom domain; paid plans remove branding and add custom domain support.

faqs.question: Who owns Weebly?
faqs.answer: Weebly was acquired by Square, now part of Block, Inc., in 2018.

faqs.question: Can I sell products with Weebly?
faqs.answer: Yes, built-in ecommerce is available on every plan, including the free tier.

faqs.question: Does Weebly integrate with Square point-of-sale?
faqs.answer: Yes, Weebly has deep integration with Square, letting in-person and online sales sync through one system.

faqs.question: Is Weebly good for beginners?
faqs.answer: Yes, its drag-and-drop editor is designed for non-technical users building their first website.

faqs.question: Can I use my own domain with Weebly?
faqs.answer: Yes, on paid plans; the free plan uses a weebly.com subdomain.

faqs.question: Does Weebly support blogging?
faqs.answer: Yes, a built-in blogging engine is included on Weebly sites.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, extensible terminal-based IRC client built for speed, scripting, and lightweight remote use.
enrichment.seo_title: WeeChat Review: Free Open-Source Terminal IRC Client
enrichment.seo_meta_description: WeeChat overview: free open-source, extensible ncurses IRC client with scripting support, SASL authentication, and a relay protocol for mobile clients.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: WeeChat, styled by its project as WeeChat and commonly written weechat.org, is a free and open-source Internet Relay Chat client created by Sebastien Helleu in June 2003 out of a desire for an IRC client with features that were not available at the time. It is released under the GNU General Public License version 3 or later and has been in continuous active development since its creation, making it one of the longer-lived open-source IRC clients still in wide use. WeeChat is designed to be light, fast, and extensible, running primarily through a default ncurses terminal interface, though its relay plugin allows other interfaces, such as the Glowing Bear web client or mobile relay apps, to connect to a running WeeChat session. The core architecture separates a lightweight base from independent, individually loadable and unloadable plugins, with the IRC plugin being the most prominent, supporting multiple simultaneous servers and networks, proxy support, IPv6, SASL authentication, DCC file transfers, and a nicklist. WeeChat supports scripting in eight languages, including Python, Perl, Ruby, Lua, Tcl, Guile, JavaScript, and PHP, with a built-in script manager for browsing and installing community-written scripts that add features like notifications, logging, bots, and custom formatting. The interface supports 256 colors, customizable status bars, smart message filtering, and horizontal or vertical window splitting for monitoring multiple channels at once. WeeChat runs on most major platforms, including Linux, the BSDs, macOS, Solaris, Haiku, and Windows, and it is commonly run persistently on a remote server inside tmux or screen so users maintain an always-on IRC presence they can reconnect to from any device. As a terminal-based, keyboard-driven tool with a text-based configuration system, WeeChat has a steeper learning curve than graphical chat clients and is primarily aimed at developers, system administrators, and other technically inclined users who value speed, scriptability, and persistent remote sessions over a graphical interface. Because it is entirely free and open source, there is no pricing tier or commercial edition; all functionality is available to anyone who installs it.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is WeeChat
paragraphs: WeeChat is a free, open-source Internet Relay Chat client created in 2003 and designed to be light, fast, and highly extensible. It runs primarily through a terminal-based ncurses interface, making it popular with developers and system administrators who prefer keyboard-driven, text-based tools.

Its plugin architecture separates a small core from independently loadable plugins, the most important of which handles IRC connectivity, while a relay plugin lets other interfaces, including web and mobile clients, connect to a running WeeChat session remotely.
WeeChat is commonly run on a remote server inside a terminal multiplexer like tmux or screen, giving users a persistent, always-connected IRC presence they can reattach to from any device.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The IRC plugin supports multiple simultaneous servers and networks, proxy support, IPv6, SASL authentication, DCC transfers, and a nicklist, covering the functionality most IRC power users need.

Scripting support spans eight languages, including Python, Perl, Ruby, Lua, Tcl, Guile, JavaScript, and PHP, with a built-in script manager for browsing and installing community scripts that add bots, notifications, and custom behavior.
The interface offers 256-color support, customizable status bars, smart filtering, and horizontal or vertical window splits so multiple channels can be monitored at once.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: WeeChat is entirely free and open source under the GPL-3.0-or-later license, with no paid tier, subscription, or commercial edition.
All core functionality, plugins, and scripting capabilities are available to anyone who downloads and installs the software.
The only associated cost is optional: hosting a remote server if a user chooses to run WeeChat persistently in the cloud rather than locally.

features.title: SASL Authentication
features.description: Secure authentication support alongside SSL/TLS encrypted connections.

features.title: Smart Filtering and Window Splits

features.title: 256-Color Support
features.description: Rich terminal color support and customizable status bars for a tailored interface.

features.description: Connect mobile or web clients such as Glowing Bear to a running WeeChat session remotely.

features.title: Plugin Architecture
features.description: A lightweight core with independent, individually loadable and unloadable plugins.

pros: Completely free and open source under the GPL-3.0-or-later license
Extremely lightweight, fast, and low resource usage
Highly scriptable and extensible for power users
Runs persistently on remote servers via tmux or screen for an always-on IRC presence
Long track record of active development since 2003
cons: Steep learning curve for users unfamiliar with terminal-based tools
No native graphical interface; relies on ncurses or third-party relay clients
Primarily focused on IRC, with other protocols requiring community plugins or bridges
Text and command-based configuration can be intimidating for beginners

faqs.question: Is WeeChat free?
faqs.answer: Yes, WeeChat is entirely free and open source under the GPL-3.0-or-later license.

faqs.question: What platforms does WeeChat run on?
faqs.answer: Linux, macOS, the BSDs, Windows, and other Unix-like systems including Solaris and Haiku.

faqs.question: Can I use WeeChat on mobile?
faqs.answer: Not natively, but its relay protocol supports mobile and web clients such as Glowing Bear that connect to a running WeeChat session.

faqs.question: Does WeeChat support scripting?
faqs.answer: Yes, in Python, Perl, Ruby, Lua, Tcl, Guile, JavaScript, and PHP, with a built-in script manager.

faqs.question: Is WeeChat only for IRC?
faqs.answer: Its core is protocol-agnostic, but the built-in and primary supported plugin is for IRC; other protocols require community plugins or bridges.

faqs.question: Who created WeeChat?
faqs.answer: Sebastien Helleu created WeeChat in June 2003, and it continues to be actively developed by its open-source community.

faqs.question: Does WeeChat support secure connections?
faqs.answer: Yes, including SSL/TLS encryption and SASL authentication.

enrichment.short_description: AI developer platform for experiment tracking, model evaluation, and MLOps used by machine learning and GenAI teams to build, monitor, and govern models.
enrichment.seo_title: Weights and Biases Review: Pricing, Features, and MLOps Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Weights and Biases overview: experiment tracking, model registry, and LLM evaluation with Weave. Pricing plans, key features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Weights and Biases, also known by its product domain wandb.ai, is an AI developer platform founded in 2017 by Lukas Biewald, Chris Van Pelt, and Shawn Lewis, and headquartered in San Francisco, California. The company began with an experiment tracking tool built to help teams train machine learning models, an approach that reportedly grew out of work supporting model builders at OpenAI, and has since expanded into a broader MLOps and AI developer platform used by machine learning and generative AI teams at both startups and large enterprises. At its core, Weights and Biases lets data scientists and ML engineers log training metrics, hyperparameters, and system performance during model training runs, then visualize and compare those runs through interactive dashboards and shareable reports. Its Model Registry provides versioning, lineage tracking, and governance for trained model artifacts and datasets, helping teams manage which model versions are used in production. A newer component called Weave extends the platform beyond traditional model training into large language model application development, offering tracing, evaluation, and scoring tools for GenAI applications so teams can monitor prompt performance, catch regressions, and compare model or prompt versions. The platform integrates with major machine learning frameworks and tools, including PyTorch, TensorFlow, Keras, Hugging Face, scikit-learn, OpenAI, and LangChain, and includes a Sweeps feature for automated hyperparameter optimization. Weights and Biases offers a free tier intended for individual and academic use, providing unlimited projects and teams alongside 5GB of monthly storage, though the free tier explicitly disallows corporate use. The Pro plan, aimed at early-stage teams and organizations with fewer than 50 employees, starts at $60 per month and adds more model seats, unlimited team collaboration, priority support, and expanded storage and data ingestion limits, with additional cloud storage available at $0.03 per gigabyte. Enterprise plans are custom-priced and billed annually upfront, adding single-tenant deployment, HIPAA compliance, customer-managed encryption keys, single sign-on, custom roles, and audit logging; a self-hosted deployment option is also available for organizations with stricter infrastructure requirements. The company has grown to roughly 300 employees and became part of CoreWeave.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Weights and Biases
paragraphs: Weights and Biases is an AI developer platform used by machine learning and generative AI teams to track experiments, manage model versions, and evaluate AI applications. It was founded in 2017 by Lukas Biewald, Chris Van Pelt, and Shawn Lewis and is headquartered in San Francisco.

What began as an experiment tracking tool for model training has grown into a broader MLOps platform, now spanning traditional model development as well as large language model application evaluation through its Weave product line.
The platform is used by both individual researchers and enterprise ML teams, with deep integrations into the tools and frameworks that make up a typical machine learning workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Experiment tracking lets teams log metrics, hyperparameters, and system information during training, then compare runs visually to understand what changes improve model performance.

The Model Registry adds versioning, lineage tracking, and governance for models and datasets, while Weave brings tracing, evaluation, and scoring capabilities purpose-built for LLM and GenAI applications.
Sweeps automate hyperparameter optimization, and native integrations with PyTorch, TensorFlow, Hugging Face, OpenAI, and LangChain let teams adopt the platform without changing their existing stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: A free tier is available for individual and academic use, including unlimited projects and teams and 5GB of monthly storage, though it prohibits corporate use.

The Pro plan starts at $60 per month and targets early-stage teams under 50 employees, adding more model seats, unlimited team collaboration, priority support, and larger storage and data ingestion allowances, with overage storage billed at $0.03 per gigabyte.

Enterprise plans are custom-priced and billed annually, adding single-tenant deployment, HIPAA compliance, SSO, custom roles, and audit logs, and a self-hosted deployment option is available for organizations with stricter data residency needs.

pros: Industry-standard experiment tracking used widely across research and enterprise ML teams
Deep, ready-made integrations with major ML frameworks and tools
Generous free tier for individuals and academic researchers
Extends beyond model training into LLM and GenAI application observability via Weave
Strong collaboration features including shared dashboards and reports
cons: Costs scale quickly for teams needing more storage or data ingestion
Learning curve for advanced features such as sweeps and artifact lineage
Self-hosted and enterprise deployment requires more setup and custom pricing
Free tier explicitly prohibits corporate or commercial use

faqs.question: Is Weights and Biases free?
faqs.answer: Yes, there is a free tier for individuals and academic use with 5GB of monthly storage, but corporate use requires a paid plan.

faqs.question: Who founded Weights and Biases?
faqs.answer: Lukas Biewald, Chris Van Pelt, and Shawn Lewis founded the company in 2017.

faqs.question: What is Weave in Weights and Biases?
faqs.answer: Weave is a toolkit within the platform for tracing, evaluating, and monitoring LLM and GenAI applications.

faqs.question: Does Weights and Biases support self-hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes, self-hosted and single-tenant enterprise deployment options are available.

faqs.question: What frameworks does it integrate with?
faqs.answer: PyTorch, TensorFlow, Keras, Hugging Face, scikit-learn, OpenAI, LangChain, and other common ML tools.

faqs.question: Is there an academic discount?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free Pro-tier academic license is available for qualifying students and researchers.

faqs.question: What company owns Weights and Biases now?
faqs.answer: Weights and Biases became part of CoreWeave.

faqs.question: Does it support hyperparameter tuning?
faqs.answer: Yes, through built-in Sweeps for automated hyperparameter optimization.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted Trello-style kanban board for team task and project management.
enrichment.seo_title: Wekan Review: Free Open-Source Kanban Board (Trello Alternative)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Wekan overview: free open-source, self-hosted Trello-style kanban board with Trello import, swimlanes, REST API, and GDPR-friendly self-hosting.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Wekan is a free, open-source kanban board application designed as a self-hosted alternative to Trello, letting teams manage tasks and projects on their own infrastructure rather than a third-party SaaS platform. It was originally created around 2015 by Maxime Quandalle under the name LibreBoard before being renamed Wekan, and since December 2016 the project has been led by maintainer Lauri Ojansivu in Finland, supported by a community of roughly 180 contributors. Wekan is built on a JavaScript stack using the Meteor framework for both its front end and back end, and it deploys through common methods including Docker containers and Snap packages, making it accessible to organizations that want to run it on their own servers or private cloud. Functionally, Wekan closely mirrors Trello's board, list, and card structure, and it supports direct import of existing Trello boards, which lowers the barrier for teams migrating away from a commercial SaaS product. Core features include drag-and-drop cards across lists, swimlanes for organizing work by category or team, labels, checklists, due dates, file attachments, comments, and user assignments, along with notifications for mentions and changes and templates for standardizing board structures across projects. Wekan supports both public and private boards with configurable visibility, and it exposes a REST API for integrating with other tools and building custom automations or webhooks. Because Wekan is self-hosted, organizations retain full control over where their data lives, which makes it a common choice for privacy-conscious teams, educational institutions, nonprofits, and government bodies that need to satisfy data sovereignty or GDPR requirements that a third-party SaaS kanban tool cannot guarantee. There is no official first-party hosted SaaS version of Wekan from the core project, so most users must install and maintain their own instance, though some third-party providers offer managed hosting or paid commercial support as an add-on. As open-source software, Wekan carries no license fees, and its cost is limited to the infrastructure and administrative time needed to run and maintain a self-hosted instance.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Wekan
paragraphs: Wekan is a free, open-source kanban board tool built as a self-hosted alternative to Trello. It was originally created around 2015 as LibreBoard by Maxime Quandalle and has been led since December 2016 by maintainer Lauri Ojansivu, with contributions from around 180 developers.

The application is built on a JavaScript and Meteor stack and deploys through Docker or Snap, letting organizations run it on their own servers or private infrastructure rather than relying on a third-party SaaS provider.
Wekan's board, list, and card structure closely mirrors Trello, and it supports direct import of existing Trello boards to simplify migration for teams switching platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core kanban functionality includes drag-and-drop cards, swimlanes, labels, checklists, due dates, attachments, comments, and user assignments, giving teams a familiar way to visualize and track work in progress.
Notifications alert users to mentions and changes, and templates let teams standardize board structures across projects to reduce setup time.
A REST API supports integrations and custom automations, and configurable public or private board visibility gives administrators control over who can see and edit each board.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Wekan is completely free and open source, with no license fees for the software itself. Costs are limited to the infrastructure needed to host and maintain a self-hosted instance.
There is no official first-party managed SaaS version from the core Wekan project, though some third-party companies offer paid managed hosting or commercial support contracts.

Because Wekan can be self-hosted entirely within an organization's own infrastructure, it can be run in a way that is fully GDPR-compliant, which is a key reason privacy-focused organizations choose it over commercial kanban SaaS tools.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Programmatic access for integrations, webhooks, and custom automations.

pros: Completely free and open source with no per-user licensing fees
Full data control through self-hosting, useful for privacy and GDPR-sensitive organizations
Familiar Trello-like interface that eases migration from Trello
Active community and frequent releases
Can run on modest hardware or small servers
cons: Requires self-hosting and server maintenance with no fully managed first-party SaaS option
Interface feels less polished than commercial alternatives like Trello or Asana
Smaller app and integration ecosystem than commercial competitors
Commercial support is a limited, paid add-on rather than a built-in offering

faqs.question: Is Wekan free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Wekan is open source and free to self-host, with no license fees for the software.

faqs.question: Can I import boards from Trello?
faqs.answer: Yes, Wekan supports importing existing Trello boards directly.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host Wekan?
faqs.answer: Typically yes, since there is no official first-party hosted SaaS version, though some third parties offer managed hosting.

faqs.question: What technology is Wekan built on?
faqs.answer: A JavaScript and Meteor framework stack for both the front end and back end.

faqs.question: Is Wekan GDPR-compliant?
faqs.answer: When self-hosted on your own infrastructure, it can be run in a fully GDPR-compliant manner since you control the data.

faqs.question: Does Wekan have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, it exposes a REST API for integrations and automation.

faqs.question: Who maintains Wekan today?
faqs.answer: Lauri Ojansivu has led development since December 2016, supported by a community of contributors.

enrichment.short_description: AI voice generation and text-to-speech platform offering realistic, licensed AI voices for content creators, L&D teams, and enterprises.
enrichment.seo_title: WellSaid Labs Review: AI Voice Generator Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: WellSaid Labs overview: 120+ realistic AI voices, commercial usage rights, Adobe integrations, pricing plans, pros, cons, and frequently asked questions.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: WellSaid Labs is an AI voice generation and text-to-speech company that spun out of the Allen Institute for AI (AI2) in Seattle, with the company generally cited as founded in 2018, and is headquartered in Seattle, Washington. The platform, marketed under WellSaid and reachable at wellsaidlabs.com, converts written text into realistic-sounding spoken audio using a library of more than 120 AI voices, each modeled on recordings by licensed, real voice actors rather than scraped or unlicensed audio, which the company positions as a differentiator for legally clear commercial use. Users can adjust tone, pacing, pitch, and emphasis to fine-tune how a script is delivered, and finished audio can be exported in multiple formats, including MP3, WAV, OGG, and accompanying caption or text files, with audio fidelity scaling up to 48 kHz on mid-tier plans and up to 96 kHz on Enterprise. WellSaid integrates directly with Adobe Premiere Pro and Adobe Express, letting video editors and content creators generate and insert voiceovers without leaving their editing workflow. The product is aimed primarily at corporate learning and development teams producing eLearning narration, marketers and content creators producing video and podcast voiceovers, and enterprises that need a consistent, branded voice at scale across many pieces of content. Pricing starts with a free trial offering a small amount of downloadable and generated audio per month with no commercial usage rights, followed by paid individual plans: Starter at $19 per month (or roughly $10 per month billed annually) with full commercial rights and unlimited generation within a monthly download cap, and Pro at $49 per month (or about $33 per month billed annually) with a larger download allowance, unlimited projects, and the Adobe Express integration. Team plans are billed annually, with Business priced at $160 per month per user for up to five seats and including a shared team workspace and live chat support, and a custom-priced Enterprise tier that adds all supported languages, translation, SSO, dedicated customer success management, and the highest audio fidelity. Because pricing is entirely subscription-based beyond the limited free trial, WellSaid is positioned as a premium, commercially-licensed alternative to lower-cost or open-source text-to-speech tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is WellSaid Labs
paragraphs: WellSaid Labs is an AI voice generation company that spun out of the Allen Institute for AI in Seattle and is generally cited as founded in 2018. It converts written text into realistic spoken audio using a library of more than 120 AI voices modeled on licensed recordings from real voice actors.

The platform is aimed at teams that need commercially usable voiceovers at scale, including corporate learning and development, marketing, and video production teams, and it emphasizes legal clarity around commercial usage rights since its voices are licensed rather than scraped.
WellSaid integrates directly into common creative workflows through plugins for Adobe Premiere Pro and Adobe Express, letting users generate voiceovers without switching tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users can select from 120-plus AI voices and adjust tone, pacing, pitch, and emphasis to control how a script sounds, then export finished audio in MP3, WAV, OGG, or caption/text formats.
Higher-tier plans unlock higher audio fidelity, up to 48 kHz on Pro and up to 96 kHz on Enterprise, along with team workspaces and collaborator seats for Business and Enterprise customers.
Direct integrations with Adobe Premiere Pro and Adobe Express let video editors generate and insert AI voiceovers directly inside their existing editing tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: A free trial offers a limited amount of downloadable and generated audio with no commercial usage rights, intended for evaluation rather than production use.

Individual paid plans start with Starter at $19 per month (about $10 per month billed annually) with full commercial rights, and Pro at $49 per month (about $33 per month billed annually) with a larger download allowance and the Adobe Express integration.

Team plans are billed annually: Business costs $160 per month per user for up to five seats with a shared workspace, and Enterprise is custom-priced with all languages, translation, SSO, and a dedicated customer success manager.

pros: High-quality, natural-sounding AI voices with clear commercial licensing
Simple interface with a minimal learning curve for content creators
Direct integrations with Adobe Premiere Pro and Adobe Express
Enterprise-grade features such as SSO and dedicated support on top tiers
Voices licensed from real actors, reducing legal ambiguity around usage
cons: No permanent free tier with commercial usage rights; only a limited trial
Fewer total voices and languages than some larger text-to-speech competitors
Business and Enterprise pricing can be expensive for small teams
Less emphasis on custom voice cloning compared to some rivals

faqs.question: Is WellSaid Labs free?
faqs.answer: There is a free trial with limited generation minutes and no commercial rights; paid plans start at the Starter tier.

faqs.question: Can I use WellSaid voices commercially?
faqs.answer: Yes, Starter, Pro, Business, and Enterprise plans all include full commercial usage rights.

faqs.question: Does WellSaid integrate with video editing tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with Adobe Premiere Pro and Adobe Express.

faqs.question: How many voices does WellSaid offer?
faqs.answer: More than 120 AI voices across a range of styles and accents.

faqs.question: Who founded WellSaid Labs?
faqs.answer: WellSaid Labs spun out of the Allen Institute for AI (AI2) in Seattle and is generally cited as founded in 2018.

faqs.question: What audio quality does WellSaid support?
faqs.answer: Up to 48 kHz on the Pro plan and up to 96 kHz on the Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: Does WellSaid offer team collaboration?
faqs.answer: Yes, Business and Enterprise plans include shared team workspaces and collaborator seats.

faqs.question: Where is WellSaid Labs based?
faqs.answer: The company is headquartered in Seattle, Washington.

enrichment.short_description: Popular large file transfer and sharing service for sending photos, videos, and design files by link or email.
enrichment.seo_title: WeTransfer Review 2026: Pricing Plans and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: WeTransfer overview: send large files by link or email, Free, Starter, and Ultimate plans, Portals for collaboration, pricing, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: WeTransfer is a large file transfer and sharing service founded in 2009 by Rinke Visser, Bas Beerens, and Ronald Hans (also known as Nalden), and headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands. It was created to make sending large files, such as photos, videos, and design assets, as simple as possible without requiring recipients to create an account, and it became especially popular among creative professionals in design, photography, video, and marketing for sharing project files with clients and collaborators. The core product lets users upload files and send them either as a shareable download link or directly via email, with options for password protection, expiration windows, and download notifications to keep transfers secure and trackable. Beyond basic transfers, WeTransfer offers Portals, dedicated pages for collecting files or feedback from multiple people, and Reviews, a tool for gathering structured feedback and approvals on creative work, both aimed at agencies and freelancers managing client collaboration. As of WeTransfer's most recent pricing structure, the company operates a three-tier model: a Free plan that allows up to 10 transfers or 3GB of total transfer volume within any rolling 30-day period, with individual transfers capped at 2GB when sent without an account; a Starter plan priced at $8 per month that raises the rolling 30-day transfer volume to 300GB; and an Ultimate plan priced at $23 per month (or about $19 per month when billed annually) that removes the rolling volume cap entirely, supporting unlimited transfers of up to 1TB each, along with custom branding for transfer pages and emails and unlimited access to Portals and Reviews. This replaced an earlier Pro and Premium plan structure that was consolidated during a 2024 pricing overhaul. Because free transfers expire after a set period, WeTransfer functions primarily as a transfer tool rather than a long-term storage solution, though paid plans add persistent cloud storage alongside transfer capability. WeTransfer's combination of a frictionless free tier, a recognizable brand among creative professionals, and creative-collaboration add-ons like Portals and Reviews differentiates it from more enterprise-focused file-sharing and sync tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is WeTransfer
paragraphs: WeTransfer is a large file transfer and sharing service founded in 2009 in Amsterdam, built around the idea of making it as simple as possible to send large files like photos, videos, and design assets without requiring recipients to create an account.

It became especially popular among creative professionals such as designers, photographers, and videographers for sharing project files with clients and collaborators, thanks to its minimal, frictionless interface.
Beyond basic transfers, WeTransfer has expanded into adjacent creative-collaboration tools, including Portals for collecting files or feedback and Reviews for structured approval workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users can send files as a shareable download link or directly by email, with options for password protection, expiration windows, and download notifications to track and secure transfers.
Portals give agencies and freelancers a dedicated page for collecting files or feedback from clients, while Reviews provide structured tools for gathering approvals on creative work.
Paid plans add persistent cloud storage in addition to transfer capability, along with custom branding for transfer pages and outgoing emails.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Free plan allows up to 10 transfers or 3GB of transfer volume within any rolling 30-day period, with individual transfers capped at 2GB when sent without an account.

The Starter plan costs $8 per month and raises the rolling 30-day transfer volume to 300GB, while the Ultimate plan costs $23 per month, or about $19 per month billed annually, and removes the rolling volume cap, supporting unlimited transfers of up to 1TB each.

Ultimate also adds custom branding for transfer pages and emails and unlimited use of Portals and Reviews, replacing the older Pro and Premium plan tiers that were folded into it during a prior pricing overhaul.

features.description: Send files up to 1TB per transfer on the top-tier paid plan.

features.description: Receive alerts when recipients download a shared file.

features.title: Reviews and Approvals
features.description: Collaborative feedback and approval tools for creative files.

pros: Extremely simple, minimal-friction interface for sending large files
Generous free tier that requires no account for basic transfers
Popular and trusted brand among creative professionals
Useful add-ons like Portals and Reviews for creative collaboration
No restrictive file-type limits for common creative assets like video and design files
cons: Free plan has tight transfer limits within a rolling 30-day window
Transfers expire, which can be a problem for long-term sharing or storage
Fewer granular admin and security controls than enterprise file-sharing tools
Ads and branding appear on free-tier transfer pages

faqs.question: Is WeTransfer free?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free plan supports smaller transfers within a rolling 30-day limit; larger, higher-volume transfers require a paid plan.

faqs.question: How large a file can I send with WeTransfer?
faqs.answer: Free transfers are capped at 2GB per send; the Ultimate paid plan supports transfers up to 1TB.

faqs.question: Do WeTransfer files expire?
faqs.answer: Yes, free transfers typically expire after a set period unless the sender upgrades to a paid plan.

faqs.question: What is a WeTransfer Portal?
faqs.answer: A dedicated page for collecting files or feedback from multiple people, available on paid plans.

faqs.question: Is WeTransfer secure?
faqs.answer: It offers password protection and expiring links; paid plans add more control over branding and access.

faqs.question: Where is WeTransfer based?
faqs.answer: WeTransfer is headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands.

faqs.question: Can I brand my WeTransfer page?
faqs.answer: Yes, custom branding for transfer pages and emails is available on paid plans.

faqs.question: Does WeTransfer offer storage, not just transfers?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid plans include cloud storage in addition to transfer capability.

enrichment.short_description: AI-assisted no-code and low-code platform for building production-ready web apps and internal tools on top of any backend.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (paid plans from about $20/month for Essential)
enrichment.seo_title: WeWeb Review: No-Code Web App Builder Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: WeWeb overview: AI-assisted no-code web app builder with visual editor, native backend, Figma import, code export, pricing plans, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: WeWeb is a no-code and low-code platform for building production-ready web applications, founded by Florian Briou, Marc Fabre, and Raphael Goldsztejn and headquartered in Paris, France, with the company generally cited as founded around 2020. WeWeb started as a visual front-end builder and drag-and-drop editor for creating dashboards, portals, and SaaS product interfaces on top of an existing backend, differentiating itself from all-in-one no-code app builders by focusing on the front end and connecting to virtually any data source, including Supabase, Xano, and custom REST or GraphQL APIs. The platform later expanded into a full-stack offering with its own native backend, called WeWeb Tables, a Postgres database with a spreadsheet-style user interface, auto-generated CRUD APIs, visual backend logic workflows, built-in authentication, and file storage, letting teams build an entire application, front end and back end, without leaving WeWeb. In 2025 the company introduced WeWeb AI, which generates user interface components and layouts from natural-language prompts and has been updated frequently since launch, and the platform also supports importing designs directly from Figma to speed up UI development. Developers can add custom logic and workflows visually in the editor, and for teams that want more control, WeWeb supports code export and GitHub sync so applications are not permanently locked into the platform. WeWeb's pricing is structured around seat plans that determine editor access and building capabilities, and separate hosting plans for deployment. The Free seat plan includes a limited AI token allowance and full access to the visual editor. The Essential plan, priced at roughly $16 to $20 per month depending on currency and promotional period, unlocks GitHub sync, a larger AI token allowance, and custom domain support. The Pro plan, at roughly $42 to $50 per month, adds unlimited seats and a larger AI token allocation for teams building and maintaining more complex projects together. A Partner plan, priced per user, is designed for agencies and freelancers doing client work, allowing them to join client workspaces for free, transfer projects, and earn referral commissions. Hosting plans, which are required in addition to a paid seat plan to deploy live applications, range from a free tier through Launch, Grow, and Scale tiers for higher-traffic production apps. WeWeb is commonly used by startups building internal tools and admin dashboards, no-code agencies building client-facing SaaS products, and product teams validating ideas through rapid prototyping.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is WeWeb
paragraphs: WeWeb is a no-code and low-code platform for building production-ready web applications, founded by Florian Briou, Marc Fabre, and Raphael Goldsztejn and headquartered in Paris, France. It started as a visual front-end builder that connects to virtually any backend, including Supabase, Xano, and custom APIs.

The platform has since expanded into a full-stack offering with its own native backend, WeWeb Tables, a Postgres database with a spreadsheet-style interface, auto-generated APIs, visual workflows, authentication, and storage, letting teams build both the front end and back end of an application in one place.

WeWeb AI, introduced in 2025, generates UI components and layouts from natural-language prompts, and the platform also supports importing designs directly from Figma to speed up interface development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The visual drag-and-drop editor lets users build responsive web app interfaces without writing code, while custom logic and workflows can be added visually for more advanced app behavior.
WeWeb connects to almost any backend, including Supabase, Xano, and REST or GraphQL APIs, or teams can use WeWeb's own native Postgres-based backend for a fully self-contained stack.
For teams that want more control, WeWeb supports code export and GitHub sync, reducing vendor lock-in compared to fully closed no-code platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: WeWeb's pricing is split into seat plans, which govern editor access and building capabilities, and hosting plans, which govern deployment of live applications.

The Free seat plan includes a limited AI token allowance and full visual editor access; the Essential plan runs roughly $16 to $20 per month and adds GitHub sync, more AI tokens, and custom domain support; the Pro plan runs roughly $42 to $50 per month and adds unlimited seats and a larger AI token allocation.

A Partner plan, priced per user, is aimed at agencies and freelancers doing client work, and separate hosting tiers, from a free tier through Launch, Grow, and Scale, are required in addition to a paid seat plan to deploy production applications.

features.title: Visual Drag-and-Drop Editor
features.description: Build responsive web app user interfaces without writing code.

features.title: WeWeb AI
features.description: Generate UI components and layouts from natural-language prompts.

features.title: Native Backend (WeWeb Tables)
features.description: A Postgres database with a spreadsheet-style UI, auto-generated CRUD APIs, and visual backend workflows.

features.title: Any-Backend Connectivity
features.description: Connect to Supabase, Xano, REST, or GraphQL APIs instead of or alongside WeWeb's native backend.

features.title: Figma Import
features.description: Convert Figma designs directly into working application user interfaces.

features.title: Workflows and Logic
features.description: Add custom application logic and automation visually within the editor.

features.title: Code Export and GitHub Sync
features.description: Export application code and sync with GitHub for developer control and reduced lock-in.

features.title: Built-In Auth and Storage
features.description: User authentication and file storage included out of the box.

pros: Combines no-code speed with real code export and developer flexibility
Works with virtually any backend, including Supabase and Xano, or its own native backend
AI-assisted UI generation speeds up building interfaces
Figma-to-app workflow is useful for design-led teams
Reduced vendor lock-in through code export and GitHub sync
cons: Steeper learning curve than pure no-code tools for non-technical users
Complex backend logic may still require a separate tool unless using WeWeb Tables
Combined seat and hosting plan pricing can add up quickly for larger teams
Smaller community and ecosystem than more established platforms like Bubble or Webflow

faqs.question: Is WeWeb free?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free seat plan is available with a limited AI token allowance and full visual editor access.

faqs.question: What backends does WeWeb support?
faqs.answer: Supabase, Xano, REST and GraphQL APIs, and its own native Postgres-based backend called WeWeb Tables.

faqs.question: Can I export code from WeWeb?
faqs.answer: Yes, code export and GitHub sync are available on paid plans, reducing vendor lock-in.

faqs.question: Does WeWeb use AI?
faqs.answer: Yes, WeWeb AI can generate UI components and layouts from prompts and imported Figma designs.

faqs.question: Is WeWeb good for internal tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is commonly used to build internal dashboards, portals, and SaaS front ends.

faqs.question: Who founded WeWeb?
faqs.answer: Florian Briou, Marc Fabre, and Raphael Goldsztejn founded WeWeb, which is headquartered in Paris, France.

faqs.question: Does WeWeb require coding knowledge?
faqs.answer: No coding is required for basic use, though custom logic and integrations benefit from technical familiarity.
